name: From Langon to Bordeaux
description:
On this last course, we are on something that looks like an adventure. Fording, sand, roots, castles and other curiosities are on the program.

Langon is a dormitory town on the outskirts of Bordeaux. It is therefore very well served by train. You leave Langon by the south going up towards Sauternes, known for its eponymous wine. The next section goes down to Budos and its medieval castle. The roads are winding and after Landiras you taste the first joys of the hidden paths along the road.

Saint-Morillon has an excellent little café called “À l’improviste”, warm and friendly, it is perfect for enjoying a refreshment or a hot drink depending on the season.

Once past La Brède, a small surprise trail awaits you. On the program of sand and water, something to delight your transmissions, but above all, something to find a child's soul on his bike. The broom of the planes of the aerodrome is then heard

The end of this route literally cuts through the woods and fields to find Bordeaux, passing some beautiful properties in the Pessac-Léognan appellation. Since there are only a few kilometers left, do not hesitate to stop for a visit, or a tasting, in moderation to finish the trip safely.
region_intro: Gravel biking around the Garonne-Kanal traverses southwestern France, offering a mix of flat, traffic-free paths and unpaved sections. The landscape features expansive agricultural areas, including vineyards and farmland, alongside woodlands. Routes often run between the canal and the Garonne River, passing through historical towns and past 19th-century engineering works. The region's terrain is generally flat along the canal towpaths, with some routes featuring moderate elevation changes. Best gravel bike trails around the Garonne-Kanal The most popular gravel bike trail is Gravel in the Val d'Aran – Prüedo per Valarties, a 19.3 miles (31.0 km) trail that takes 3 hours 22 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the Val d'Aran region with notable elevation changes. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is From Portets to Bordeaux, an easy 22.3 miles (35.9 km) path. This route follows the canal and river, connecting towns with a generally flat profile. Local gravel bikers also love the Brax Castle – Bouconne State Forest loop from Toulouse-Saint-Cyprien-Arènes, a 36.1 miles (58.1 km) trail leading through varied terrain near Toulouse, often completed in about 3 hours 18 minutes. Gravel biking around the Garonne-Kanal is defined by canal-side paths, agricultural landscapes, and historical towns. The network offers options for easy rides along greenways as well as more challenging routes with greater elevation. The routes in Garonne canal are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 90 reviews. More than 600 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Garonne canal's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many gravel bike trails are available around the Garonne Canal?
A: There are over 100 gravel bike trails in the Garonne Canal region, offering a wide variety of experiences. These include 14 easy routes, 49 moderate routes, and 46 difficult routes, catering to all skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on gravel bike trails around the Garonne Canal?
A: The terrain is quite varied. You'll find mostly flat, traffic-free paths along the canal, often shaded by plane trees, which are ideal for relaxed rides. However, there are also unpaved and more challenging gravel sections, particularly in areas with greater elevation changes like the Val d'Aran. Routes often traverse agricultural areas, vineyards, woodlands, and run between the canal and the Garonne River.

Q: Are there family-friendly gravel biking options along the Garonne Canal?
A: Yes, many sections of the Garonne Canal's greenway are asphalted and largely traffic-free, making them suitable for families and beginners. These paths offer a safe and pleasant environment for relaxed exploration. Look for routes described as 'easy' for the most family-friendly experiences.

Q: What are some challenging gravel bike routes in the Garonne Canal area?
A: For those seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1764670136/gravel-in-the-val-d-aran-prueedo-per-valarties" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel in the Val d'Aran – Prüedo per Valarties&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation changes. Other difficult routes include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1078480424/from-langon-to-bordeaux" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Langon to Bordeaux&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070226198/from-cerons-to-bordeaux" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Cérons to Bordeaux&lt;/a&gt;, which feature more varied terrain and ascents.

Q: Are there any circular gravel bike routes available?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23515256" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bank of the Touch – Small Bridge over the Touch loop from Canal du Midi&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through varied terrain. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/35847449" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brax Castle – Bouconne State Forest loop from Toulouse-Saint-Cyprien-Arènes&lt;/a&gt;, offering a moderate challenge.

Q: What historical towns or landmarks can I explore along the gravel bike trails?
A: The Garonne Canal region is rich in history. You can cycle through medieval towns like Marmande, La Réole, and Damazan, or visit Moissac with its UNESCO World Heritage-listed abbey. The impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/929964" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Agen Aqueduct&lt;/a&gt; is a notable engineering marvel. Many routes connect to major cities like Bordeaux and Toulouse, which offer numerous historical sites.

Q: Are there natural attractions or viewpoints along the Garonne Canal gravel trails?
A: Absolutely. The canal often runs alongside the Garonne River, providing scenic views. You'll pass through expansive vineyards and farmland, and near Bordeaux, you might spot traditional fishing huts known as 'carrelets'. For specific natural highlights, consider visiting &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3302745" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lake Major of Colomers&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4645821" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bordeaux lake&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for gravel biking around the Garonne Canal?
A: The Garonne Canal is generally pleasant for cycling from spring through autumn. The paths are often shaded by plane trees, offering relief during warmer weather. Spring and early autumn provide comfortable temperatures and beautiful scenery, with vineyards in bloom or harvest. Winter cycling is possible, but check local weather conditions as some paths might be affected by rain.

Q: Can I bring my dog on gravel bike trails in the Garonne Canal region?
A: While many outdoor areas in France are dog-friendly, it's always best to check specific local regulations for the exact section of the canal or any nature reserves you plan to enter. Generally, dogs on a leash are welcome on public paths, but ensure they are well-behaved and that you clean up after them.

Q: What amenities like cafes or accommodation are available along the routes?
A: The Garonne Canal route is dotted with charming towns and villages, many of which offer cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. Major cities like Bordeaux, Agen, and Toulouse, connected by the canal, provide extensive amenities. Smaller towns like Marmande and Moissac also have facilities for cyclists.

Q: How can I access the gravel bike trails, particularly regarding parking or public transport?
A: Access to the Garonne Canal trails is generally good. Many towns along the canal offer parking facilities. For public transport, regional trains often serve towns along the canal, and some may allow bicycles, though it's advisable to check specific train line policies and peak hour restrictions. The canal's connectivity between cities like Bordeaux and Toulouse also makes it accessible by various transport links.

Q: What do other gravel bikers say about the trails in the Garonne Canal region?
A: The Garonne Canal region is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 200 reviews. Reviewers often praise the picturesque landscapes, the historical towns, and the unique experience of cycling alongside the impressive engineering works of the canal. Many appreciate the mix of easy, traffic-free sections and more challenging gravel paths.

Q: Are there any longer gravel bike routes for multi-day trips or extended rides?
A: Yes, the Garonne Canal is part of the larger Canal des 2 Mers route, connecting the Atlantic to the Mediterranean, which offers extensive possibilities for multi-day trips. For a longer single-day ride, consider &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/37762457" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Small Bridge over the Touch – Bank of the Touch loop from Beauzelle&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 112 km.
tips: Imposing medieval fortress which now houses a wine estate. The castle comprises two buildings, the old one, of which only a few foundations remain, and the new one, remodeled during the reign of Henri IV and remodeled in the 17th century.
Incredible ruins of the 14th century Budos castle, in the middle of the vineyards. Under restoration by Adichats association for about thirty years.
A passage to join a pleasant DFCI track in all seasons
The fording to be done thoroughly like kids! Even in winter in the cold it goes and it's funny!
